body.u: Today was my eighteenth birthday. Looking back on my life I reflected over all those years and what I had done with them.

After leaving school at 17, I went for my first proper job. It was working for the ministry of Defence in London while at school I had visited the careers office several times, it was from there I obtained the information about the ministry of Defence, and by the time I had left school was pretty clear on what sort of work I would be doing.

After working there as a clerical officer for a few years I decided that it wasn’t really my sort of work. So I started to think about becoming a nurse, something I would have liked to have done from a child.

A few months after leaving I started training to be a nurse, my five ‘o’ levels which I had acquired were helpfullhelpful. This work I found more to my likeingliking because it involved people, and not just sitting around in an office all day.

Five months after starting my training I was married at 20. I found it difficult at first carrying on my training and having a husband. Finally three years training I became a fully qualified nurse, and also a mother. I was a bit depressed at first, I remember after all I had been slogingslogging away for three years to become a nurse, then I became one and had to give it up. But when I saw theretheir faces I didn’t mind so much.

After three years I went back to work, by this time I found it a releafrelief to get out. I found the work more interesting then when I had left it, because I had no worries. The children were at nursery school, and my husband at work as a builder we had a lovely home, and I felt very happy. Life carried on like this until I retired, after sending my children out into the world.
Life had basically been good to me, apart from a few ups and downs.
